ECON1220_ch7_4:
Price Control – Intervening the Market
People may believe the price determined by 
the free-market is not “desirable” or 
“appropriate”, so intervene!
Rent control (price ceiling) 
Price ceiling: A legal maximum on the price at 
which a good can be sold
Minimum wage (price floor)
Price floor: A legal minimum on the price at which 
a good can be sold

ECON1220_ch7_16:
Can rent ceiling helps/benefits buyers?
Gain (seems): Buyers, (if) who can rent 
apartments, and (if) pay a lower price
Less buyers can rent apartment under rent 
ceiling: Qs’ < Q*
Bring us more fairness?
Those “Lucky” buyers may have to pay black-
market rent or “grey-area payments” – possible 
that most of CS will be transferred (back) to 
sellers as a result
DWL means a total loss to society
Some mutually benefited trade cannot happen
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
16
ECON1220_ch7_17:
Explanatory Note – Summary
IF rent ceiling can be effectively enforced by the 
government, then P’ would be the rental payment that 
would be paid by buyers and received by the sellers, 
and Qs’ will be the no. of apartment available in the 
market 
Qs’ < Q*, then DWL happens (reduction in social 
welfare as measured size of social surplus)
Since price mechanism has been restricted by the 
government, non-price rationing methods will appear 
to “solve” the shortage problem. If such non-price 
rationing methods do not allocated apartments to 
highest WTP buyers, size of social surplus would 
further reduce (further loss on top of DWL) 
ECON1220 L1 – Market, Demand and Supply
17
ECON1220_ch7_18:
Explanatory Note – Summary
However, we know that government’s enforcement is 
never perfect, Black/Grey market appears as a non-
price rationing method under the existence of shortage
At Qs’, the “black market payment + P’ “ can be as 
large as the WTP of buyers, which mean:
Black market payment transfer surplus back to sellers (If 
just a redistribution of surplus, it does not affect efficiency)
Highest WTP buyers will “win” by (biggest amount) black 
market payment, and be allocated with apartments (non-
price rationing method, but eventually is related to 
price/money)
Therefore, black market payment allows efficient 
allocation of apartment (highest WTP buyers), though 
surplus will be largely transferred into sellers’ hands 
ECON1220 L1 – Market, Demand and Supply
18
ECON1220_ch7_19:
Explanatory Note – Summary
Think further, with black/Grey market payment, the 
rental received by sellers could be larger than P’, so 
quantity supplied of apartment could be higher than 
Qs’ , therefore DWL reduced (closer to Q*, improve of 
efficiency)
In extreme case, if government does nothing to enforce 
the rent ceiling, P* and Q* could still be the market 
outcome
Black market serves as a mean to improve the 
efficiency of allocation of apartments under rent 
control 
Another example:  iPhone 8 (resell to mainland by 
those Hong Kong buyers who won the “lucky-draw” by 
Apple

ECON1220_ch7_20:
Explanatory Note – Summary
Corruption may improve the resource allocation at a 
particular point of time when the price signal of free-
market is prevented from working
However, corruption also means a special type of 
social network to be created, which means people will 
spend resources on building up the network for 
corruption
Resources used to build up such a network instead 
can be used for other productive purposes

Minimum Wage on Teenage and 
Elderly Workers
Minimum Wage on Teenage and 
Elderly Workers
Economy contains NOT a single market but 
many different markets for different types of 
workers
Minimum wage on skill and experience 
workers has no impact, because market 
equilibrium wage of these markets is above 
the minimum wage (not effective)
Teenage or elderly workers would be more 
likely to affected, given equilibrium wage of 
some of them is below the minimum wage 
(effective)

Minimum Wage – Short and Long Run
In the Long-run
Minimum wage (higher than equilibrium 
wage) will attract more people to join the job 
market
Quit school earlier
From employer perspective, higher labor cost 
may force some companies out of business 
and therefore reduce labor demand
Unemployment (surplus of labor) will become 
more serious

ECON1220_ch7_35:
Taxation
Government needs funding to operate
Legal system, infrastructure, military forces
Government uses taxation as a policy tool
Sales tax on cigarette to deter smoking
Tariffs to deter the amount of imports 
Progressive income tax arrangement for 
redistribution purpose
ExamplEs: Corporate tax, income tax, sales 
tax, tariff (import tax), etc.

ECON1220_ch7_36:
Taxation
Questions we may have:
How tax affects our behaviors?  Including 
sellers and buyers, how tax affects supply 
and demand? How tax affects the free-
market outcome?
When gov’t is going to impose a tax, who is 
going to bear the tax burden?
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
36
ECON1220_ch7_37:
Taxation
Tax incidence: the division of the burden of a 
tax between buyers and sellers
When an item is taxed, its price might rise by 
the full amount of the tax, by a lesser amount, 
or not at all:
If the price rises by the full amount of the tax, 
buyers pay the tax
If the price rises by a lesser amount than the tax, 
buyers and sellers share the burden of the tax
If the price doesn’t rise at all, sellers pay the tax

that?

ECON1220_ch7_40:
Tax on a good
If HK Gov’t is to levied a tax on iPhone12, will 
the tax be collected from sellers or buyers?
Any difference if the tax is collected from sellers 
or buyers? 
From administrative perspective:
Number of sellers and buyers: Buyers > Sellers
Lower administrative cost if tax levied on sellers
Tax can be % of a good’s price or a specific 
amount for each unit sold (per-unit tax)
Here, for simplicity, we analyze per-unit tax 
HERE

Which goods or services should gov’t tax 
to raise the revenue it needs? 
One answer:  Those with the smallest DWL
When is the DWL small vs. large?

measures how much QD (or QS) changes 
when P changes 
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
66
ECON1220_ch7_67:
Question (2)
Would the DWL of a tax be larger if the tax 
were on:
A. Breakfast cereal or sunscreen
B. Hotel rooms in the short run or hotel rooms 
in the long run
C. Groceries of meals at fancy restaurants
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
67
ECON1220_ch7_68:
Question (2) - Answer
A. Breakfast cereal or sunscreen
Breakfast cereal has more close substitutes 
than sunscreen, so demand for breakfast 
cereal is more price-elastic than demand for 
sunscreen.  
So, a tax on breakfast cereal would cause a 
larger DWL than a tax on sunscreen.
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
68
ECON1220_ch7_69:
Question (2) - Answer
B. Hotel rooms in the short run or hotel rooms 
in the long run
The price elasticities of demand and supply 
for hotel rooms are larger in the long run than 
in the short run. 
So, a tax on hotel rooms would cause a 
larger DWL in the long run than in the short 
run.
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
69
ECON1220_ch7_70:
Exercise (2) - Answer
C. Groceries of meals at fancy restaurants
Groceries are more of a necessity and 
therefore less price-elastic than meals at 
fancy restaurants.
So, a tax on restaurant meals would cause a 
larger DWL than a tax on groceries.

ECON1220_ch7_71:
How Big Should the Government Be?
If you want your government to provide more 
“public services”, the government needs 
more resource/money or the government has 
to collect more taxes from citizens
However, higher taxes usually means a 
bigger DWL
For example, income tax (on labor) is major 
source of tax revenue of government
If the income tax is as big as 50%, will you work 
hard/long hours?
Similarly, if you earn $1, but can only “enjoy” 
$0.5, it means a “disincentive”
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
71
ECON1220_ch7_72:
How Big Should the Government Be?
Taxes also serve redistribution purpose, 
that is more taxes are collected from wealthy 
and then those taxes will spend on 
items/services that to be enjoyed by 
everyone or some items/services for low-
income group especially
Too “much” of redistribution may lead to dis-
incentive to work hard
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
72
ECON1220_ch7_73:
How Big Should the Government Be?
I don’t mean we should not help the poor 
(although it is normative), but the idea of 
“welfare-state”, that is the government should 
provide all kinds of welfare to its citizens will 
lead to a stagnant economy
Example: China adopted “collective” farm/factory 
system before, that is all people worked in a 
farm/factory, all eat in communal dining place, 
government provide all other services of daily life
Result was: No one work, and economy 
collapsed, which lead to economic reform in 1979
After economic reform, the GDP of China has 
growing rapidly
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
73
ECON1220_ch7_74:
How Big Should the Government Be?
As discussed before, incentive is important!
You may then say “balance” is important!
In a developed country with high level of GDP 
(output level), it is more “able” to bear the 
DWL due to the redistribution
However, for a developing country with low 
level of GDP, which barely enough to support 
the subsistence of its citizens, DWL due to 
the idea of welfare-state could be too big and 
could be destructive

ECON1220 L1 –Ten Principles of Economics

ECON1220_ch1_9:
1. People Face Trade-offs
SCARCITY: The amount of an item is 
insufficient to satisfy the want of a person or 
want of people, then scarcity happens for the 
item for an individual or the society
“Scarcity” is about “availability” AND “want” 
TOGETHER
A box of eggs usually has limited number of bad 
eggs (limited in availability), but if no one wants 
(or wants is less than availability), there is no 
scarcity for bad eggs!!!
People’s want is larger than the availability 
and people are willing to “pay” to obtain it

something for it
The item is valuable
When an item is not scarce: 
Are you willing to pay for it?

alternative, it is not part of the cost (Ch1-

be part of the cost

differentiated products) and many buyers
Perfect competition: Many sellers (of identical 
products) and many buyers

and buyers

When satisfying the following assumptions, 
we call the type of market: The Perfect 
Competitive Market
It is used as a benchmark against other “market 
structures”: Monopoly, oligopoly, etc. 
It is the “most efficient” market structure

Buying and selling of a “homogeneous” good
Many buyers and sellers
Full information

Therefore, buyers and sellers are “Price 
Taker”

Price-Taker Market
Competition in a perfect competitive market is 
called Perfect Competition
Perfect competitive market also called 
competitive market

ECON1220_ch4_49:
Excess Supply and Demand
When Price is above the Equilibrium Price
“Excess Supply”: Qs > Qd
Sellers cannot sell as much as they want
The appearance of “imbalance” between 
buyers and sellers affects the bargaining 
power between them
As a seller (of many), what will you do?
Lower price to attract more buyers & to buy more
As price drops, reducing the amount you want to 
supply
Insist not to lower price? You lose to other sellers
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply
49
ECON1220_ch4_50:
Excess Supply and Demand
As a buyer, what will you do?
Bargain with sellers for lower price
As price drops, you may consider to buy more
When a seller resist to lower price, you turn your 
back to the seller
Price will tend to move downwards towards 
the equilibrium price (P*)
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply

ECON1220_ch4_51:
Excess Supply and Demand
If Price is below the equilibrium price
“Excess Demand”: Qd > Qs
Buyers cannot buy as much as they want
As a buyer (of many), what will you do?
As a seller (of many), what will you do?

1) Before getting to equilibrium, the “path” of

ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply

ECON1220_ch4_54:
Reminder (Again)
Demand-Supply framework is convenient!
It summarizes factors that affect market price 
and equilibrium into two groups: (1) Demand-
side and (2) Supply-side
The intersection of demand and supply 
determines the so-called equilibrium, which is 
a simple concept and easy to work, however:
(1)The interaction (i) Between buyers and 
sellers; (ii) Among buyers; and (iii) Among 
sellers are the “forces” behind the scene
For a good economist, you never forget the 
human thinking and behavior behind
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply
54
ECON1220_ch4_55:
Reminder (Again)
At the same time, please remember that 
Demand-Supply framework says nothing 
about:
How long it takes to reach the equilibrium price 
and quantity
The “path of the price change” before reaching 
the equilibrium
Demand-Supply is “too” simple to tell the 
above, so trade-off:
Theory of everything vs Theory of something
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply
55
ECON1220_ch4_56:
How Price Allocates Resources
Demand-supply model describes how price is 
determined in a free-market
“What price does” is ALSO important: 
Allocation of scarce resources
Who get the scare goods : In market, scarce 
goods go to “most willing to pay” buyers (for 
whom to produce)
Final product market provides sellers incentive on 
what they produce and factor markets on how to 
produce
That is the three fundamental questions of 
economics!
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply
56
ECON1220_ch4_57:
How Price Allocates Resources
Price: Organizing production activities to 
satisfy the highest valued wants (resources 
allocation) in a Decentralized way, which 
means three fundamental economics 
questions are solved without a “Commander”
Everyone just perform whatever to maximize 
their own benefits in the market
ECON1220 L1 – Market forces of Demand and Supply
57
ECON1220_ch4_58:
How Price Allocates Resources
Driven by own interests, through the 
interaction/negotiation/bargaining, wants and 
other information of each player were 
“gathered” together, “processed” and hence 
determine the equilibrium, at which scare 
resources are put into the most efficient uses
“The Use of Knowledge in Society”, 1945, 
American Economic Review

himself
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PoTVSsxiwGc
Free Market Masters Friedrich Hayek
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LrzKDKPvg4Y

own thinking!!!

ECON1220_ch6_3:
Competition
When scarcity exists in the society, we must 
compete with each others
We must decide who can get the scarce items
Competition is unavoidable given the scarcity, 
however, the determination of “how to 
compete” would be a bit complicated
Why not using “boxing flight”?

ECON1220_ch6_4:
Competition
Boxing flight?  You may think it is brutal and 
create injuries easily to competitors (costly)
But strong classmates may prefer
Then, how about a “running race”?
Fast-running classmates may prefer
Why not “beauty”?

ECON1220_ch6_5:
Competition
Many other ways you can name:
Lining up; Lottery; Seniority; Power; Exam; 
Highest price (market); … …, etc.
Different “rules” of “competition” may lead to 
different “winners”, and
If you have the authority to set the rule, which 
one will you choose?
Will you pick the one that gives you the most 
favorable outcome? (self-fish motive)

Efficiency; Fairness; Ethics; Or just “I like it” ~~~
From the perspective of positive Economics, 
we said free-market is “good” for its 
efficiency, and what is “efficiency”?
In economics, efficiency is measured by the 
gain from trade – the total of Consumer 
Surplus and Producer Surplus
Social Surplus = Consumer Surplus + 
Producer Surplus

ECON1220_ch6_18:
In the diagram, total surplus is maximized 
when quantity exchanged = Q*
Overproduction: Q*+1 th unit, cost of production 
is larger than the value of buyer
Underproduction: Q*-1 th unit, value of buyer is 
larger than the cost of production
Total Surplus = (WTP – amount paid by 
buyers) + (Amount received by sellers – Cost 
to sellers)
Total surplus = WTP – Cost to sellers

ECON1220_ch6_19:
At Q*, the society can maximize or exhaust 
the potential gain from trade
Efficiency is achieved when total surplus is 
maximized
Note that the price(s), for example, P* determines 
how the gain from trade (WTP-MC) is divided
From economic perspective, no matter under 
what form of competition, if Q* can be 
achieved, they are efficient
Efficiency

ECON1220_ch6_20:
Can planned economy achieve Q*?
If you know the info of demand and supply
If you have the authority to order how many 
unit should be produced and exchanged
In order to maximize the total surplus, you 
can order the producer to produce Q* and 
delivery these Q* to highest WTP buyers
Planned Economy: Social Planner
ECON1220 L1 – Consumers, Producers, and the Efficiency of market

ECON1220_ch6_21:
Planned Economy: Social Planner
Is it possible for you to know the info of 
demand and supply?
Survey? Even you have the resources to do 
that for everyone, people can cheat you 
about their WPT and cost of production 
(Asymmetric information)
Unless you have a “super machine” that can 
read people’s mind!
With the “super machine”, is it enough?
Still we need the social planner is 
“benevolent”!!!
ECON1220 L1 – Consumers, Producers, and the Efficiency of market
21
ECON1220_ch6_22:
Market allocation
On the other hand, Adam Smith’s invisible 
hand:  Market incorporates all information 
of buyers and sellers
Through market (or price signal), the what, 
how and for whom to produce are answered
Resources are diverted to its best uses 
(achieving efficiency)
Under perfect competitive market, the 
equilibrium quantity is Q* with equilibrium 
price at P* !!!!!! Market allocation is 
efficient!!!
ECON1220 L1 – Consumers, Producers, and the Efficiency of market
22
ECON1220_ch6_23:
Small summary
CS, PS and social surplus provides us a 
measurement for society’s welfare gain from 
economic activities (production & exchange) 
and perfect competitive market maximizes
social surplus in a decentralized way
Note: This measure is on the “total size”, but 
nothing to do with “fairness”: How the total 
gain is shared among different ones
Social planner can achieve the same as 
market “theoretically”, but Information 
problem likely to make “social planner 
solution” impossible 
ECON1220 L1 – Consumers, Producers, and the Efficiency of market

ECON1220_ch6_24:
Small summary
Now, you have more information to judge by 
yourself: Principle 6 of your textbook, Ch.1
Markets are usually a good way to organize 
economic activity
But, just “usually” !!!
Sometimes, even some markets fail to deliver 
P* and Q*:
When market is not Perfect Competitive
Market failure – Public good & Externalities
Market is not almighty! Economists know 
clearly about it!!!

Room for “government” to “step in”, if we 
don’t like certain outcomes arise under free-

ECON1220_ch3_3:
Introduction
When people will perform an exchange?
(1) We have the same item; 
However, (i) maybe different values in 
association with it; (ii) You have a lot of it, so your 
marginal value is low
An exchange will bring benefit to each party
(2) You produce an item at a lower cost than 
me, and my value of an item is higher than 
your cost
Specialization and exchange
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
3
ECON1220_ch3_4:
Introduction
Benefit from (2) is much more significant 
Road map:
(1) What determines the pattern of 
specialization
(2) Specialization brings benefit
(3) Mutual beneficial (voluntary) exchange 
can happen
Before the above, let’s talk about what is 
Primary Factors of Production in Economics
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
4
ECON1220_ch3_5:
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
Primary Factors of Production
“Primary factors of production” are provided 
by households, which include
Labor: work and effort from human beings
Capital: capacity to produce goods & services
Land: gifts from nature; and 
Entrepreneur: Risk taking in production (residual 
claimant)!
Sometimes, factors of production can substitute 
each others
Labor and machine: Labor / Capital intensive
5
ECON1220_ch3_6:
Availability of “Labor”
Labor NOT “a person”, but his effort
“Labor” is MORE than “population”
NOTE, “incentive” is important to induce a 
person works effectively and efficiently (level 
of effort provided)
Institution and compensation system: Right to 
choose a job; To enjoy the reward from effort
Example in US – Incentive is important!!! Slavery 
was allowed to buy back their freedom, if they 
work hard / productive to accumulate enough 
money.  Why?
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
6
ECON1220_ch3_7:
Availability of “Labor”
Besides correct incentive: Machinery or tool 
can improve the productivity of labor
In developed countries, we usually can 
observe correct incentive systems and high 
amount of machinery stock is high (can be 
measured by capital per capita)
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
7
ECON1220_ch3_8:
Availability of “Entrepreneur”  &“Land”
Entrepreneur to distinguish between “labor 
effort” and “risk-taking behavior”
Land (natural resources) was regarded as 
fix in old days, but may not be true nowadays
Land, oil reserves, iron ore, coal, etc.
Still, most Land is created by nature
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
8
ECON1220_ch3_9:
Availability of “Capital”
Capital creation (or formation) is an 
interesting and important process
An economic decision: Trade-off of current 
and future consumption
As mentioned, accumulation of capital is 
highly related to productivity and the level of 
living standard of an society
ExamplE: Fisherman
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
9
ECON1220_ch3_10:
ExamplE: Fisherman and Fishing
Wing likes fish and does fishing for a living
At first, he can only do that by bare hands!
Fishes escape easily, and he could do fishing 
at shallow water
Tough life !!!
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
10
ECON1220_ch3_11:
Fisherman and Fishing
To improve the situation:
To buy a fishing net !!!
He can do much better with it
Note: spending money for it means a 
reduction in consumption today and wish for 
higher consumption in future.  Trade-off!!!
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
11
Yeah! Yeah! Yeah!
ECON1220_ch3_12:
Wing sacrificed some of his current 
consumption (saving) to buy fish net  
(investment - capital creation)
With the fishing net, he can get enough to eat 
and to sell (return to capital) 
Increase of labor productivity with more capital
In this example, besides supplying his own 
labor, Wing is the entrepreneur as well
Any profit or loss from the production is borne by 
Wing

ECON1220_ch3_16:
Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF) is 
always used to illustrate the gain from 
specialization in Economics:
Why and how specialization can increase total 
output of a society / a group of individuals
Also important: Idea on how the gain from 
specialization divided between individuals
What is a good model/theory?
PPF model is simple.  Yet simple but provides 
many insights
Production – Specialization

ECON1220_ch3_17:
Check Point (2)
Under which of the following condition(s) will 
specialization between Person A and B leads 
to a larger total output for them?
1) Person A is good at producing X and B is 
good at Y
2) Person A is good at producing both X and Y
3) Person B is good at producing both X and Y
4) All of the above
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
17
ECON1220_ch3_18:
Check Point (2)
The correct answer is 4
A person is good at X means he uses less 
resources in providing X
Under 1) Person A has absolute advantage 
on X and B on Y
Under 1) and 2), though one person has 
absolute advantage on both X and Y.  
However, difference in their marginal costs 
lead to comparative advantage
Comparative advantage is enough for 
specialization to be beneficial

how many goods can be produced

Factor of production = 8 hours of labor

ECON1220_ch3_28:
Absolute Advantage
A person has “Absolute Advantage” at a 
task over another person if the person needs 
less resources (time and/or other resources) 
to perform it
Albert has absolute advantage (use less time 
than Wing) on Meat
Wing has absolute advantage (use less time than 
Albert) on Potato
Should they SPECIALIZE?
Yes, why not!  It bring benefit to both of them
How to show it is mutual beneficial?

ECON1220_ch3_29:
Absolute vs Comparative Advantage
However, should “Absolute Advantage” be 
used to judge whether specialization would 
be beneficial?
NO…NO…NO…
In fact, the condition should be Comparative 
Advantage, which does not depend on 
Absolute Advantage
Let me show you in the following example…

ECON1220_ch3_33:
Comparative Advantage
Opportunity cost of producing Potato (Meat)
Wing use 15 min to produce 1 oz  Potato, this 15 
min is enough to produce 0.25 oz Meat on the 
other hand: 1 P to 0.25 M (1M to 4P)
Albert use 10 min to produce 1 oz Potato, this 10 
min is enough to produce 0.5 oz Meat on the 
other hand: 1 P to 0.5 M (1M to 2P)
Opportunity cost of producing Potato is 
represented by the steepness of the slope of 
one’s PPF

ECON1220_ch3_34:
Comparative Advantage
A person has “Comparative Advantage” over 
another person on a task, if the person’s 
“Opportunity Cost” of performing that task is 
“lower” than that of the other person
Opportunity Cost, NOT amount of resources 
(which is Absolute Advantage!)
If Wing has CA on Potato over Albert, it 
means SIMILARITY Albert has CA on Meat 
over Wing
Wisdom:  May be you are not the best from 
ANY aspects, you still can find a spot for 
yourself

ECON1220_ch3_45:
Division of gain from specialization –
Exchange Ratio
Division of gain from specialization –
Exchange Ratio
Albert has to give up 1.5 unit of meat to 
produce 1 unit of potato by himself
If 1 unit of potato can be exchanged by 1 unit 
of meat (in the market), will Albert produce 
potato by himself?
No! “Cheaper” for Albert to produce meat and 
use meat to exchange for potato
He will be better-off by 0.5 unit of meat under 
this exchange ratio (1 Meat for 1 Potato)
Gain from specialization and exchange!!!

of Meat, will Wing still produce meat by 
himself?
No! “Cheaper” for Wing to produce Potato 
and use Potato to exchange for Meat
He will be better-off by 3 unit of Potato under 
this exchange ratio (1 Meat for 1 Potato)
Gain from specialization and exchange!!!

ECON1220_ch3_57:
Shape of PPF – Cost of Production
Cost of production changes when more of a 
good is produced:
If cost remains constant, PPF is a straight line

ECON1220_ch3_64:
Policy Discussion
Does free-trade benefit everyone?
In our example, we have Wing and Albert, 
and specialization and trade benefit both 
Wing and Albert
How about if we talk about trade between two 
countries?  Does free-trade benefit everyone 
in both countries?
The answer could be complicated
For example, when China trades with US
Both countries benefited
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
64
ECON1220_ch3_65:
Policy Discussion
Trade is the flow of goods/services between 
two countries; on the other hand, it is also 
just like both countries open its boarder and 
allow people of both countries competing with 
each others
In this sense, if China’s comparative 
advantage is in labor-intensive items (due to 
low labor cost), trade will put US labors to 
compete with the low-cost labors of China
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
65
ECON1220_ch3_66:
Policy Discussion
Therefore, although US, as a whole unit, can 
be benefited from obtaining “cheap” imports 
(less costly than producing by itself), but 
some US labors who facing the competition 
from China’s labors may get hurt
However, those US labors, who get hurt in 
labor market, can also enjoy the low-cost 
imports in their consumption at the same time
Therefore, considering the two impacts, it is 
difficult to conclude those US labors are 
benefited or not

ECON1220_ch5_5:
Elasticity
Elasticity measures how much one variable 
responds to changes in another variable, 
examples:  
1) Own Price Elasticity: Change in quantity 
demanded in response to change in price
2) Income Elasticity: Change in quantity 
demanded in response to change in income
3) Cross-Price Elasticity: Change in quantity 
demanded of A in response to change in 
price of B

ECON1220_ch5_12:
Check Point (4)
Price of gasoline increases by 30%, does Qd 
drop more in the short run or long run? Why?
Short-run, long-run: No clear definition, amount of 
time after the price change
Not much you can do in short-run: not drive, 
public transportation
Long-run: smaller cars, more fuel-efficient cars, 
live closer to where you work
Learning: Price elasticity is higher in the long 
run than the short run
ECON1220 L1 – Elasticity and Its Application
12
ECON1220_ch5_13:
Summary
The price elasticity of demand depends on:  
the extent to which close substitutes are available
whether the good is a necessity or a luxury
how broadly or narrowly the good is defined
the time horizon—elasticity is higher in the long 
run than the short run 
Generally, how easy a consumer can 
escape!!!

ECON1220_ch5_39:
Factors Affect Supply Elasticity
Resource substitution possibilities:  
“Tea-restaurant” and “High-end steak house” 
require different inputs
Chef, waiter / waitress, food ingredient, location, 
renovation, etc. are inputs
Inputs are more specialized for High-end steak 
house, and therefore input supply for high-end 
steak house will be inelastic (easy to bid up their 
prices)
The supply of high-end steak house services 
would be more inelastic than that of tea 
restaurant as supply of inputs

To set up a new plant (or to set up a new 
production line) to produce laptop computer is 
more complex than that of making T-shirt
The longer the time after price change, the 
more elastic will be the supply (higher supply 
elasticity)

HUMA1678_ch8_3:
Chapter 7 
Network Data 
7.1 
Introduction 
A network consists of a set of objects and a collection of links identifying some 
relationship between these pairs of objects [65]. Networks form a very generic 
data model with extensive applications to the humanities [92]. Networks can be 
a great way to understand connections and relationships between a wide range of 
objects or types of data. For example, one might want to explore the friendship 
relationships between people, citations between books, or network connection 
between computers. Whenever there exists a set of relationships that connects the 
objects to each other, a network can be a useful tool for visualization and data 
exploration. 
A critical step in interpreting network data is deciding exactly what elements 
correspond to the nodes (the objects) and the edges (links between the edges). For 
example, let’s say we are studying citations. A node might be an author and the 
edge may connect an author when they cite one another. The SignsAt40 project is a 
great example. They look at 40 years of feminist scholarship through network and 
text analysis, including co-citation networks. Being very clear about how nodes and 
edges are deﬁned is key to exploring networks. 
In this chapter, we start by working with network data taken from Wikipedia 
using the same set of pages we saw in the previous chapter. Wikipedia pages contain 
many internal links between pages; we collected information about each time one of 
the pages in our dataset provided a link to another page in our collection. Only links 
within the main body of the text were used. We will explore how to do produce this 
dataset using the Wikipedia API in Chap. 12. 
© The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2024 
T. Arnold, L. Tilton, Humanities Data in R, Quantitative Methods in the Humanities 
and Social Sciences, https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-62566-4_7
117

7.2 
Creating a Network Object 
We can describe a network structure with a tabular dataset. Speciﬁcally, we can 
create a dataset with one row for each edge in the network. This dataset needs one 
column giving an identiﬁer for the starting node of the edge and another column 
giving the ending node. The set of links between the Wikipedia pages is read into 
R and displayed by the following block of code. Notice that we are using the same 
values in the doc_id column that were used as unique identiﬁers for each page text 
in Chap. 6. 
page_citation <- read_csv(file.path( 
"data", "wiki_uk_citations.csv" 
)) 
page_citation

Looking at the ﬁrst few rows, we see that Marie de France has only one link (to 
Geoffrey Chaucer). Chaucer, on the other hand, has links to six other authors. As a 
starting way to analyze the data, we can see how many pages link into each author’s 
page. Arranging the data by the count will give a quick understanding of how central 
each author’s page is to the other authors, as seen in the following block of code. 
By summarizing links into each page rather than out of each page, we avoid a direct 
bias toward focusing on longer author pages. 
page_citation |> 
group_by(doc_id2) |> 
summarize(count = n()) |> 
arrange(desc(count))

Looking at the counts, we see that there are more links into the Shakespeare page 
than any other in our collection. While that is perhaps not surprising, it is interesting 
to see that Wordsworth is only two links short of Shakespeare, with 23 links. While 
raw counts are a useful starting point, they can only get us so far. These say nothing, 
for example, about how easily we can hop between two pages by following 2, 3, or 4 
links. In order to understand the dataset, we need a way of visualizing and modelling 
all of the connections at once. This requires considering the entire network structure 
of our dataset. 
Before we create a network structure from a dataset, we need to decide on what 
kind of network we will create. Speciﬁcally, networks can be either directed, in  
which case we distinguish between the starting and ending vertex, or undirected, 
in which we do not. For example, in our counts above, we took the direction into 
account for the links. Next, let’s start by treating our dataset of Wikipedia page 
links as undirected; all we want to consider is whether there is at least one way 
to click on a link and go between the two pages. Later in the chapter, we will 
show what changes when we add direction into the data. Any directed network can 
be considered undirected by ignoring the direction; undirected networks cannot in 
general be converted into a directed format. So, it will be a good starting point to 
consider approaches that can be applied to any network dataset. 
The dataset that we have in R is called an edge set [52]. It consists of a dataset 
where each observation is an edge. The hdir package provides an easy way of taking 
an edge list and returning structured data the allows us to understand the dataset as a 
network. It uses the excellent igraph internally to compute these quantities [25, 26]. 
If we apply the function hdir_network_metrics to the edge list, an R object 
containing two elements is returned. Splitting these off requires using the dollar 
sign notation and results in two datasets. 
library(hdir) 
nwork <- hdir_network_metrics(page_citation , directed = FALSE) 
node <- nwork$node 
edge <- nwork$edge 
The node dataset contains extracted information about each of the objects in our 
collection. We will describe each of these throughout the remainder of this chapter. 
Note that we also have metadata about the nodes, which is something that we can

The ﬁrst column gives a label for the row. In the next two columns, named x and y, 
is a computed way to layout the objects in two dimensions that maximizes linked 
pages being close to one another while minimizing the amount that all of the nodes 
are bunched together [32]. This is an example of a network drawing (also known as 
graph drawing in mathematics) algorithm. As with the PCA and UMAP dimensions 
in Chap. 6, there is no exact meaning of the individual variables. Rather, its the 
relationships that they show that are interesting. Using the ﬁrst three variables, we 
could plot the pages as a scatterplot with labels to see what pages appear to be 
closely related to one another. 
library(ggrepel) 
node |> 
ggplot() + 
geom_point(aes(x = x, y = y)) +  
geom_text_repel(aes(x = x, y = y, label = id)) + 
theme_void() 
Before actually looking at this plot, it will be useful to ﬁrst make some additions. 
The relationships that would be shown in this plot generally try to put pages that 
have links between them close to one another. It would be helpful to additionally 
put these links onto the plot as well. This is where the edge dataset becomes useful. 
The edge dataset contains one row for each edge in the dataset. The dataset has four 
columns. These describe the x and y values of one node in the edge and variables 
xend and yend to indicate where in the scatterplot the ending point of the edge is.

We can include edges into the plot by adding a geom layer of type geom_segment. 
This geometry takes four aesthetics, named exactly the same as the names in the 
edge dataset. The plot gets busy with all of these lines, so we will set the opacity 
(alpha) of them lower so as to not clutter the visual space with the connections. 
node |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + 
geom_segment( 
aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, alpha = 0.1 
) +  
geom_text_repel( 
aes(label = id), size = 3 
) +  
theme_void() 
The output of the plot is shown in Fig. 7.1. We can see the underlying data that 
describes the plot as well as the relationships between the pages. Notice that the 
relationship between the pages is quite different than the textual-based ones in the 
previous chapter. When using textual distances, we saw a clustering base on the 
time period in which each author wrote and the formats that they wrote in. Here, the 
pattern is driven much more strongly by the general popularity of each author. The 
most well-known authors of each era—Shakespeare, Chaucer, and Jonathan Swift— 
are clustered in the middle of the plot. Lesser-known authors, such as Daphne du 
Maurier and Samuel Pepys, are pushed to the exterior. In the next section, we will 
see if we can more formally study the centrality of different pages in our collection.
HUMA1678_ch8_8:
122
7
Network Data
Fig. 7.1 Visualization of the page-link network over the collection of British authors Wikipedia 
pages. Nodes correspond to pages and edges correspond to a link existing between the two pages; 
in other words, one page has an internal link in the body of the text to the other page. A subset of 
the nodes are labeled with the author names 
7.3 
Centrality 
One of the core questions that arises when working with network data is trying to 
identify the relative centrality of each node in the network. Several of the derived 
measurements in the nodes dataset capture various forms of centrality. Let’s move 
through each of these measurements to see how they reveal different aspects of our 
network’s centrality. 
A component of a network is a collection of all the nodes that can be reached by 
following along the edges. The nodes dataset contains a variable called component 
describing each of the components in the network. These are ordered in descending 
order of size, so component 1 will always be the largest (or at least, tied for 
the largest) component of the network. The total size of each component is the 
ﬁrst measurement of the centrality of a node. Those nodes that are in the largest 
component can, in some sense, be said to have a larger centrality than other nodes 
that are completely cut off from this cluster. All of the nodes on our network are 
contained in one large cluster, so this measurement is not particularly helpful in 
this speciﬁc case. Networks that have a single component are known as connected 
networks. All of the other metrics for centrality are deﬁned in terms of a connected
HUMA1678_ch8_9:
7.3
Centrality
123
Fig. 7.2 Visualization of the page-link network over the collection of British authors Wikipedia 
pages. Nodes are colored by their degree (the number of nodes that they are directly linked to) 
network. In order to apply them to networks with multiple components, each 
algorithm is applied separately to each component. 
Another measurement of centrality is a node’s degree. The degree of a node is 
the number of neighbors it has. In other words, it counts how many edges the node 
is a part of. The degree of each node has been computed in the node table. This is 
similar to the counts that were produced in the ﬁrst section by counting occurrences 
in the raw edge list. The difference here is that we are counting all edges, not just 
those edges going into a node. As a visualization technique, we can plot the degree 
centrality scores on a plot of the network to show that the nodes with the highest 
degree do seem to sit in the middle of the plot and correspond to a high number of 
having a large number of edges. Such a plot is produced by the following code, with 
the output shown in Fig. 7.2. 
node |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_segment( 
aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, alpha = 0.1 
) +

The degree of a node only accounts for direct neighbors. A more holistic measure-
ment is given by a quantity called the eigenvalue centrality. This metric is provided 
in the node table. It provides a centrality score for each node that is proportional to 
the sum of the scores of its neighbors. Mathematically, it assigns a set of scores . sj
for each node such that 
. sj = λ ·
⎲
i∈Neighborsj
si
The eigenvalue score, by convention, scales so that the largest score is 1. It is 
only possible to describe the eigenvalue centrality scores for a connected set of 
nodes on a network, so the computation is done individually for each component. 
For comparison, we will use the following code to plot the eigenvalue centrality 
scores of our network. 
node |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_segment( 
aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, alpha = 0.1 
) +  
geom_point(aes(color = eigen), size = 5) + 
scale_color_binned(type = "viridis", n.breaks = 15) + 
theme_void() 
The visualization in Fig. 7.3 shows a slightly different pattern compared to the 
degree centrality scores. The biggest difference is that the eigenvalue centrality is 
more concentrated on the most central connections, whereas degree centrality is 
more spread out. We will see in the next few sections that this is primarily a result 
of using a linear scale to plot the colors. If we transform the eigenvalue centrality 
scores with another function ﬁrst, we would see that the pattern more gradually 
shows differences across the entire network. 
Another measurement of centrality is given by the closeness centrality score. For 
each node in the network, consider the minimum number of edges that are needed 
to go from this node to any other node within its component. Adding the reciprocal 
of these scores together gives a measurement of how close a node is to all of the 
other nodes in the network. The closeness centrality score for a node is given as 
the variable close in our nodes table. Again, we will plot these scores with the 
following code to compare to the other types of centrality scores.

The output of the closeness centrality scores is shown in Fig. 7.4. Notice that these 
scores are slightly different than the eigenvalue centrality scores. Here, we see a 
much smoother transition from the most central to the least central nodes. We 
will look at different kinds of networks later in the chapter that illustrate further 
differences between each type of centrality score. 
The ﬁnal measurement of centrality we have in our table, betweenness centrality, 
also comes from considering minimal paths. For every two nodes in a connected 
component, consider all of the possible ways to go from one to the other along edges 
in the network. Then, consider all of the paths (there may be only one) between 
the two nodes that require a minimal number of hops. The betweenness centrality 
scores measures how many of these minimal paths go through each node (there is

some normalization to account for the case when there are many minimal paths, so 
the counts are not exact integers). This score is stored in the variable between. A

The betweenness score often tends to have a different pattern than the other 
centrality scores. It gives a high score to bridges between different parts of the 
network, rather than giving high weight to how central a node is within a particular 
cluster. One challenge with the page link network over this small set of pages is that 
we need to create a different kind of network in order to really see the differences 
between the betweenness centrality and the other types of centrality that we’ve 
discussed so far. To better understand the centrality scores, we will delve further 
into another set of networks such as co-citation and nearest neighbor networks.

7.4 
Clusters 
The centrality of a node is not the only thing that we can measure when looking at 
networks. Another algorithm that we can perform is that of clustering. Here, we try 
to split the nodes into groups such that a large number of the edges are between 
nodes within a group rather than across groups. When we created our network, 
a clustering of the nodes based on the edges was automatically performed. The 
identiﬁers for the clusters are in the column called cluster. We can visualize the 
clusters deﬁned on our Wikipedia page network using the following code. 
node |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_segment( 
aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, alpha = 0.1 
) +  
geom_point(aes(color = cluster), size = 5) + 
theme_void() 
The output of the cluster visualization is show in Fig. 7.6. We are running out of 
space to put labels on the plot. This is one major consideration when thinking 
of networks as a form of visual exploration and communication; bigger is not

HUMA1678_ch8_15:
7.5
Co-citation Networks
129
Network clusters can be very insightful for understanding the structure of a large 
network. The example data that we have been working with so far is relatively 
small and forms a larger single cluster around a few well-known authors. Because 
of the length and richness of the textual information, this set of 75 authors produced 
interesting results on its own in the previous chapter. It is also a great size to 
visualize and illustrate the core computed metrics associated with network analysis 
since it is small enough to plot every node and edge. To go farther and show the full 
power of these methods as analytic tools, we need to expand our collection. 
7.5 
Co-citation Networks 
The network structure we have been working with is a form called a citation 
network. Pages are joined whenever one page links to another. This is a popular 
method in understanding academic articles, friendship networks on social media 
(i.e., tracking mentions on Twitter), or investigating the relative importance of 
court cases. There are some drawbacks of using citation counts, however. They are 
sensitive to the time order of publication, they are affected by the relative length of 
each document, and they are easily affected by small changes. Wikipedia articles are 
continually edited, so there is no clear temporal ordering of the pages, and there is 
relatively little beneﬁt for someone to artiﬁcially inﬂate the network centrality of an 
article. The length of Wikipedia articles, though, are highly variable and not always 
well correlated with the notoriety of the subject matter. So, partially to avoid biasing 
our results due to page length, and more so to illustrate the general concept when 
applying these techniques to other sets of citation data, let’s look at an alternative 
that helps to avoid all of these issues. 
A co-citation network is a method of showing links across a citation network 
while avoiding some of the pitfalls that arise when using direct links. A co-citation 
is formed between two pages whenever a third entry cites both of them. The idea is 
that if a third source talks about two sources in the same reference, there is likely 
a relationship between the documents. We can create a co-citation dataset from 
Wikipedia by ﬁrst downloading all of the pages linked to from any of the author 
pages in our collection. We can then count how often any pair of pages in our dataset 
were both linked into from the same source. As with the other Wikipedia datasets, 
we will see how to create this collection through the API in Chap. 12. Here, we 
will load the dataset into R as a structured table. Co-citations are, by deﬁnition, 
undirected. In the dataset below, we have sorted the edges so that doc_id always 
comes alphabetically before doc_id_out. The column count tells how many third 
pages cite both of the respective articles. 
page_cocitation <- read_csv( 
file.path("data", "wiki_uk_cocitations.csv") 
) 
page_cocitation

The most central nodes in the co-citation network show a similar set of pages 
showing up in the top 10 list but with some notable differences in the relative 
ordering. Shakespeare is no longer at the top of the list. Focusing on the ﬁrst name 
on the list, there are at least 36 other authors in our relatively small set that are 
cited at least six times in the same page as John Milton. Looking into the example 
links, we see that this is because Milton is associated with most other ﬁgures of the 
seventeenth century, poets, and writers that have religious themes in their works. 
These overlapping sets create a relatively large number of other pages that Milton 
is connected to. As mentioned at the start of the section, the differences between 
citation and co-citation are somewhat muted with the Wikipedia pages because 
they are constantly being modiﬁed and edited, making it possible for pages to 
directly link to other pages even if they were originally created before them. When 
working with other citation networks, such as scholarly citations or court records, 
the differences between the two are often striking. 
7.6 
Directed Networks 
At the start of the chapter, we noted that networks can be given edges that are either 
directed or undirected. The algorithms and metrics we have looked at so far all work 
on undirected networks, and so even in the case of the original links, which do have 
a well-deﬁned direction, we have been treating the links as undirected. It is possible 
to create a network object that takes this relationship into account by setting the 
directed argument of the function hdir_network_metrics to TRUE. 
nwork <- hdir_network_metrics(page_citation , directed = TRUE) 
node <- nwork$node 
edge <- nwork$edge 
The node table contains a slightly different set of measurements. Closeness 
centrality is no longer available; eigenvalue and betweenness centrality are still 
computed but are done so without using the directions of the edges. There are now 
three different degree counts: the out-degree (number of links on the page), the in-
degree (number of links into a page), and the total of these two.

Fig. 7.8 Comparison of the in-degree and out-degree in the British authors collection of Wikipedia 
pages. Several outlying data points are labeled with the names of the corresponding author 
also has more links out to other authors than any other page. While the two metrics

other pages in our collection but has many outgoing links. It’s possible that this is 
partially a temporal effect of Orwell being a later author in the set; the page cites his 
literary inﬂuences and it’s not hard to see why those inﬂuences would not cite back 
into him. Wordsworth and Lord Byron show the opposite pattern, with more links 
into them than might be expected given their number of out links. Both of these are 
interesting observations that merit further study. 
7.7 
Distance Networks 
For a ﬁnal common type of network found in humanities research, we return to 
a task that we saw in Chap. 6. After having built a table of textual annotations, 
recall that we were able to create links between two documents whenever they are 
sufﬁciently close to one another based on the angle distance between their term-
frequency scores. By choosing a suitable cutoff score for the maximal distance 
between pages, we can create an edge list between the pages.
HUMA1678_ch8_21:
7.8 Nearest Neighbor Networks
135 
library(cleanNLP) 
edges <- anno |> 
cnlp_utils_tfidf(token_var = "lemma") |> 
hdir_tidy_angle_distance() 
|> 
filter(document1 != document2) |> 
arrange(distance) |> 
filter(distance < 0.4) |> 
select(document1 , document2) 
Using these edges, we can create a distance network. As with co-citations, the 
network here has no notion of direction, and therefore, we will create another 
undirected network. 
nwork <- hdir_network_metrics(edges) 
node <- nwork$node 
edge <- nwork$edge 
Plotting the network for the distance-based metrics shows a very different pattern 
from the citation and co-citation plots. The code below produces a plot based on the 
distance scores; the output is shown in Fig. 7.9. 
node |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_point() + 
geom_segment( 
aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, alpha = 0.1 
) +  
geom_text_repel(aes(label = id)) + 
theme_void() 
We see that, rather than one large dominant component as in the other plots, there 
are several smaller, but tightly connected, components. The output here shows 
connections that are more tightly connected to the periods in which each author 
wrote and the format of their works. In general, and unsurprisingly, this plot looks 
closer to the dimensionality visualizations that we saw in Chap. 6, which also were 
attempting to show which pages use similar sets of terms. This becomes very 
powerful for studying areas like legal and social history as well as sociology. 
7.8 
Nearest Neighbor Networks 
We ﬁnish this chapter by looking at one ﬁnal network type that we can apply to our 
Wikipedia corpus. The example here generates a network structure that is designed 
to avoid a small set of central nodes by balancing the degree of the network across
HUMA1678_ch8_22:
136
7
Network Data
Fig. 7.9 Visualization of the text distance-based network from the collection of British authors 
Wikipedia pages. Only pages with at least one link are shown and all nodes are labeled with the 
author names 
all of the nodes. This approach can be applied to any set of distance scores deﬁned 
between pairs of objects. As in the previous section, here, we will work with the 
angle distances provided by the TF-IDF scores from the texts of the pages. We will 
ﬁlter to just include the nouns, adjectives, adverbs, and verbs. It will be useful to 
store these distances as a dataset, which we do with the following code. 
dists <- anno |> 
filter(upos %in% c("NOUN", "ADJ", "ADV", "VERB")) |> 
cnlp_utils_tfidf(token_var = "lemma") |> 
hdir_tidy_angle_distance() 
|> 
filter(document1 != document2) |> 
arrange(distance) 
Rather than cutting off the distances with a ﬁxed value, we will start by taking, for 
each page, its ﬁve nearest neighbors. We determined the cutoff of ﬁve by some trial 
and error in an attempt to get a network that is not highly focused on a few central 
pages while also making sure that there are not too many disconnected components 
in the network. In the following code, we store these top-distance pairs as a new 
dataset.
HUMA1678_ch8_23:
7.8
Nearest Neighbor Networks
137
top_dists <- dists |> 
group_by(document1) |> 
arrange(distance) |> 
slice(1:5) |> 
ungroup() 
It is certainly possible to use top_dists as the edges in a network. This would 
yield a nearest neighbor network. In our experience, using the nearest neighbors 
will only give slightly different results to a hard cutoff value for distances for most 
nodes in most cases. Instead, let’s do one extra round of ﬁltering before using the 
pairs of distances. Speciﬁcally, we will perform a semi-join of the top_dists table 
into itself to only include edges that are in the nearest neighbors of each node. In 
other words, we only include an edge if both nodes are in each other’s list of top (in 
this case) 5 neighbors. In the code below, we perform this ﬁltering and then create a 
network from the resulting edges. 
edges <- top_dists |> 
semi_join(top_dists, 
by = c( 
"document1" = "document2", 
"document2" = "document1" 
)) |> 
select(document1 , document2) |> 
filter(document1 < document2) 
nwork <- hdir_network_metrics(edges) 
node <- nwork$node 
edge <- nwork$edge 
The network we have now created is called a symmetric nearest neighbors network. 
It can be constructed from any distance function that provides distances between 
pairs of objects in a dataset. Notice that the degree of every node in this case can 
never be larger than ﬁve. This stops the network from focusing on lots of weak 
connections to popular pages and focuses on links between pages that go both ways. 
Looking at the nodes with the highest eigenvalue centrality scores, we see that the 
top pages have moved around quite a bit. Shakespeare is much farther down on the 
list, with Virginia Woolf and Jane Austen rising toward the top. 
arrange(node, component , desc(eigen))

The structure of the symmetric nearest neighbors network is quite different from 
the other networks we have explored. One way to see this is by looking at the 
relationship between eigenvalue centrality and betweenness centrality. In most of 
the other networks, these were highly correlated to one another, but in this example, 
that is not the case, as shown with the following code. 
node |> 
filter(component == 1) |> 
ggplot(aes(eigen, between)) + 
geom_point() + 
geom_text_repel(aes(label = id), max.overlaps = 5) 
As shown in Fig. 7.10, there are several nodes that have a high betweenness score 
despite having a lower eigenvalue centrality score. Notably, here, we have Charlotte 
Smith, James Joyce, Christopher Marlowe, and Rudyard Kipling. These are the 
gatekeeper nodes that link other, more densely connected parts of the plot. A good 
way to understand what these nodes look like in the network is to highlight the 
gatekeeper nodes on a plot of the nodes and edges. The plot in Fig. 7.11, created by 
the following code, does just that. 
node |> 
mutate(color = if_else( 
between > 50 & eigen < 0.5, "orange", "grey85" 
)) |> 
ggplot(aes(x, y)) + 
geom_point(aes(color = color), size = 4) + 
geom_segment(aes(xend = xend, yend = yend), data = edge, 
alpha = 0.1) + 
scale_color_identity() + 
theme_void() 
Looking at the plot of the gatekeeper nodes, we see that they sit at crucial points 
that, if removed, would split the largest component of the network into multiple 
disconnected parts. Since the symmetric nearest neighbors plot avoids placing too 
many nodes all at the center of the network, the clusters resulting from the network 
are also often more interesting and uniform in size than other kinds of networks. 
Below, let’s look at the clusters in the large component of the network.

Network Data
Fig. 7.11 Visualization of the symmetric nearest neighbor network with the gatekeeper nodes— 
those with high betweenness but lower eigenvalue centrality scores—in orange 
We see that the clusters create several distinguishable groups, such as the third 
cluster of playwrights, the fourth of Gothic novelists, and the ﬁfth of early authors. 
While all of the network types here have their place, when given a set of distances, 
using symmetric nearest neighbors is often a good choice to get interesting results 
that show the entire structure of the network rather than focusing on only the most 
centrally located nodes. 
7.9 
Extensions 
We have explored some of the major areas of network analysis: network drawing, 
measures of centrality, and clustering. We have tried to give a general overview; 
however, all of these areas are far richer than what can be ﬁt into a single. For 
further study, the igraph documentation is a good place to start; it contains dozens 
of additional network drawing, centrality, and community detection algorithms. 
Beyond this, Stanley Wasserman’s text on social network analysis gives a lot of 
depth (in an applicable way) while remaining fairly accessible [92]. For a more 
technical treatment, Eric Kolaczyk’s Statistical Analysis of Network Data provides
HUMA1678_ch8_27:
7.9
Extensions
141
even more detail while still being written from the perspective of conducting applied 
data analysis [52]. 
As we noted at the beginning, being clear about the nodes and edges is key. We 
have found that the more strictly they are deﬁned, the more useful networks are as 
an analytical and visual tool for exploring and communicating humanities data. In 
digital humanities circles, Scott Weingart is known for repeating that when one has 
a hammer, everything can look like a nail [38]. Networks lend themselves to this, so 
being precise and careful is critical [1].
